agent_output:
{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "DynaPricing is a real-time dynamic pricing system for delivery services that adjusts prices based on demand levels. The CEO's description is a high-level vision statement from an all-hands meeting — not a formal specification. It is intentionally vague and leaves critical business parameters undefined.",
    "bounded_context": "Pricing",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "DynaPricing", "definition": "The product name for the real-time dynamic delivery pricing system" },
      { "term": "Demand", "definition": "A measure of delivery request volume relative to capacity — exact definition not specified (geographic? temporal? both?)" },
      { "term": "Guardrails", "definition": "Upper and/or lower bounds on price adjustments to prevent extreme pricing — thresholds undefined" },
      { "term": "Delivery Price", "definition": "The price charged to a customer for a delivery, subject to dynamic adjustment" },
      { "term": "Fully Automated", "definition": "Price adjustments occur without manual human intervention once the system is running" }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "Delivery prices must adjust in real time in response to changes in demand",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a business policy — the company could decide to batch price updates hourly or daily. 'Real time' is a design choice, not a physical or data-integrity constraint.",
      "testable_assertion": "When demand level changes, the system produces a new price within a defined latency threshold (threshold TBD)"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "When demand increases, price must increase relative to the baseline price",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is the core pricing policy — a VP could change the pricing curve or introduce exceptions (e.g., loyalty customers exempt from surges).",
      "testable_assertion": "Given demand level D2 > D1, the computed price at D2 must be >= the computed price at D1"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "When demand decreases, price must decrease relative to the baseline price",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Same reasoning as BR-2 — this is a pricing policy, not a system integrity constraint.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given demand level D2 < D1, the computed price at D2 must be <= the computed price at D1"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-4",
      "rule": "A price must never be adjusted beyond defined upper and lower guardrail bounds",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "If the system produces a price outside guardrail bounds, it has violated its own safety constraint. This is a state integrity rule — a price outside bounds is a corrupt output. The bounds themselves are policy (adjustable), but enforcement of whatever bounds are set is an invariant.",
      "testable_assertion": "For any computed price P: guardrail_min <= P <= guardrail_max"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-5",
      "rule": "The delivery price must always be greater than zero",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "A zero or negative delivery price is nonsensical in a for-profit delivery business — it represents a corrupt pricing state. You cannot charge a customer a negative amount for a delivery.",
      "testable_assertion": "For any computed price P: P > 0"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-6",
      "rule": "Price adjustments must be fully automated with no manual human approval step in the pricing loop",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Automation level is a business policy decision. The company could later decide certain thresholds require human review.",
      "testable_assertion": "A price change triggered by demand shift must complete without any human approval gate"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-7",
      "rule": "The pricing function must be monotonically correlated with demand — higher demand never produces a lower price, lower demand never produces a higher price",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a stronger restatement combining BR-2 and BR-3 as a monotonicity constraint on the pricing curve. It's a policy — the business could introduce non-monotonic strategies (e.g., demand cliffs, volume discounts at scale).",
      "testable_assertion": "The price-to-demand function f(d) must be monotonically non-decreasing across the entire demand range"
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "Demand spikes instantaneously (e.g., flash event, weather emergency)", "expected_behavior": "Guardrails must cap the price increase — system must not produce an unbounded surge price" },
    { "scenario": "Demand drops to zero", "expected_behavior": "Price must still remain above zero and within guardrail minimum — the system must not offer free deliveries" },
    { "scenario": "Demand oscillates rapidly (flapping)", "expected_behavior": "Unclear from spec — should the system dampen rapid price fluctuations or pass them through? Customer experience risk if prices change second-to-second" },
    { "scenario": "Guardrail bounds are misconfigured (min > max, or both set to zero)", "expected_behavior": "Undefined — system needs validation on guardrail configuration itself" },
    { "scenario": "Multiple overlapping demand signals (geographic + temporal + event-based)", "expected_behavior": "Undefined — no clarity on how demand is measured or aggregated" },
    { "scenario": "Customer sees one price, confirms order, but demand changed mid-checkout", "expected_behavior": "Undefined — this is a critical gap. Must a quoted price be honored for some TTL? This is the single most important missing rule." },
    { "scenario": "Regulatory price gouging laws triggered during emergencies", "expected_behavior": "Undefined — guardrails may need legal compliance teeth, not just commercial reasonableness" },
    { "scenario": "System loses access to demand data feed", "expected_behavior": "Undefined — should it freeze at last known price, revert to baseline, or halt?" }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "Direct revenue impact — dynamic pricing can increase margins during peak demand but risks customer churn if perceived as exploitative. Incorrect guardrails could either leave money on the table (too tight) or trigger PR backlash (too loose).",
    "operational": "Fully automated pricing removes human bottlenecks but introduces monitoring burden. Incorrect pricing at scale propagates instantly — no human circuit breaker by design.",
    "user": "Customer trust is the primary risk. Customers expect price stability and transparency. Surge pricing without explanation erodes loyalty. Price volatility during checkout is a conversion killer.",
    "risk": "HIGH — regulatory risk (price gouging laws in many jurisdictions), reputational risk (social media backlash on surge pricing), financial risk (misconfigured guardrails could hemorrhage money or gouge customers at scale with no human in the loop).",
    "stakeholders_affected": [
      { "group": "Customers", "impact": "Direct — they pay different prices based on timing. Fairness perception is critical." },
      { "group": "Couriers/Drivers", "impact": "Indirect — demand-based pricing likely affects delivery volume and potentially courier compensation if tied to delivery price." },
      { "group": "Finance/Revenue Team", "impact": "Must understand and forecast revenue when prices are no longer static." },
      { "group": "Customer Support", "impact": "Will receive complaints about price differences — needs visibility into why a price was charged." },
      { "group": "Legal/Compliance", "impact": "Must validate guardrails against price gouging regulations per jurisdiction." },
      { "group": "Marketing/Brand", "impact": "Surge pricing is a brand risk — needs messaging strategy." }
    ]
  },

  "confidence": {
    "level": 25,
    "reasoning": "This is a CEO's all-hands pitch, not a specification. Almost every critical parameter is undefined: what constitutes 'demand', what the guardrail thresholds are, how 'real time' is measured, whether quoted prices are honored, how demand is segmented, what the pricing curve shape is, and whether regulatory constraints apply. The only things stated with certainty are the directional relationship between demand and price, the desire for automation, and the existence of guardrails. Everything else is inference or gap.",
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "Prices go up when demand goes up (explicitly stated)",
      "Prices go down when demand goes down (explicitly stated)",
      "System is automated (explicitly stated)",
      "Some form of guardrails will exist (explicitly stated)"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "Definition and measurement of 'demand' — geographic, temporal, capacity-based?",
      "Guardrail thresholds — what are the min/max bounds?",
      "Price quote honoring — is there a TTL on a quoted price?",
      "Pricing curve shape — linear, exponential, step function?",
      "Latency definition of 'real time'",
      "Regulatory/legal compliance requirements",
      "Demand data source and reliability model",
      "Interaction with existing pricing (base price determination)",
      "Whether discounts, promotions, or loyalty tiers interact with dynamic pricing"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "A baseline/static price exists that dynamic pricing adjusts from",
      "Demand is quantifiable and available as a real-time signal",
      "Guardrails include both upper and lower bounds",
      "Price must always be positive (inferred from 'profitable')",
      "The monotonic demand-price relationship has no exceptions",
      "'Fully automated' means zero human approval in the pricing loop, not zero human oversight"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "The definition of 'demand' is completely undefined. Is it request volume per zone per hour? Ratio of requests to available couriers? Absolute or relative to historical baseline? The entire pricing model depends on this definition and it is absent.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Engineering", "Finance", "Product"],
      "options": ["Request volume per geographic zone per time window", "Request-to-courier ratio (supply/demand)", "Deviation from historical baseline"],
      "recommendation": "Define demand as request-to-available-courier ratio per zone — this captures both demand and supply, which is what actually drives pricing pressure in logistics."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "Guardrail bounds are mentioned but no thresholds are defined. Without concrete bounds, BR-4 is unimplementable and untestable.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Finance", "Legal", "Product"],
      "options": ["Percentage cap on surge (e.g., max 3x base)", "Absolute dollar cap", "Jurisdiction-specific caps tied to regulation"],
      "recommendation": "Define as percentage of base price with jurisdiction-specific overrides for legal compliance. Suggest max 3x as starting point with legal review."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "No mention of price quote honoring. If a customer sees $8 and clicks 'confirm' but demand shifted the price to $12 during checkout, what happens? This is the #1 source of customer complaints in surge pricing systems.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Customers", "Customer Support", "Legal"],
      "options": ["Honor quoted price for a TTL window (e.g., 5 minutes)", "Always charge current price at confirmation time", "Show price range instead of exact price"],
      "recommendation": "Honor the quoted price for a defined TTL (e.g., 3-5 minutes). Charging a different price than what was shown is a trust-destroying anti-pattern and may violate consumer protection laws in some jurisdictions."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "No mention of regulatory constraints. Many jurisdictions have price gouging laws that activate during emergencies. A fully automated system with no human override could violate these laws at scale.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Legal", "Compliance", "Executive"],
      "options": ["Add emergency override capability", "Build jurisdiction-aware guardrails", "Both"],
      "recommendation": "Mandatory legal review before launch. Build jurisdiction-aware guardrails with an emergency kill switch that freezes prices to baseline."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "No failure mode defined. What happens when the demand signal is unavailable, stale, or corrupted? A fully automated system with no fallback could produce nonsensical prices.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Engineering", "Operations", "Customers"],
      "options": ["Fall back to baseline price", "Freeze at last known good price", "Circuit-break and alert operations"],
      "recommendation": "Fall back to baseline price and alert operations. Never let a missing signal produce an unbounded price."
    }
  ],

  "rejection_reasons": []
}
